narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 57.4% chance that you would not pass—crossing the threshold we use to make such decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, like your undergraduate GPA of 3.40 and LSAT score of 32, alongside your Year 1 and Year 3 grade deciles, family income in the lower-middle bracket, and your plan to attend full-time. The model weighed these factors against patterns from thousands of past students, focusing on what best signals bar exam success. The biggest influence pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 1 decile ranking of 1st, which is much lower than the 6th decile average among those who passed, signaling early academic struggles that often carry over. Next, your Year 3 decile of 2nd also nudged the prediction toward failure, again falling well below the typical 6th decile for successful bar takers, showing limited improvement over time. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA stood out positively, as it's higher than the 3.24 average for those who passed, helping pull the prediction somewhat toward success. While your LSAT score is below the 37 average for passers and your family income bracket is less common among them—where middle and upper-middle incomes dominate—along with your full-time status aligning with most who succeed, these played smaller roles here. Compared to admitted students, who generally show stronger early and consistent rankings plus higher test scores, your profile highlighted risks in foundational performance that the model prioritized. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, just a data-driven flag on bar passage odds. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or higher, strengthening your early-grade foundation through targeted prep courses, and gathering letters highlighting your resilience and upward trends. Reapplying after addressing these could shift your predicted odds favorably, opening doors at schools like ours. You're clearly driven—many have turned similar profiles around with focused effort.
